title: Decorated iced biscuits
subtitle: Vanilla butter biscuits with royal icing in two consistencies
excerpt: Crisp vanilla butter biscuits decorated with royal icing in two consistencies: a stiff outline icing piped around the edge of each biscuit, then a thinner flood icing poured inside and spread smooth. The flood icing dries to a hard, glossy surface that can be painted or further decorated. The biscuits themselves are a simple all-butter recipe that holds its shape perfectly during baking.
sourceNotes: Decorated sugar biscuits iced with royal icing are documented in British and European confectionery literature from the eighteenth century onward. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, freely available at Project Gutenberg), describes royal icing for biscuit and cake decoration. The two-stage outline-and-flood icing method is described in British domestic cookery and confectionery guides published before 1928.

recipe.yieldDescription: 24 to 30 biscuits depending on cutter size
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked, undecorated biscuits. Defrost at room temperature and decorate once fully dry.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Biscuits keep undecorated in an airtight tin for up to a week. Decorated biscuits keep for up to 5 days in a single layer in an airtight box once the icing is fully dry.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. Bake for 10 to 14 minutes depending on thickness; the biscuits should be barely golden at the edges for the cleanest icing surface.

recipeTools.notes: For the outline icing. A small round nozzle gives the cleanest result.

glossaryTerms.definition: Royal icing thinned with water until it is pourable and flows level when spread inside an iced outline. The test for flood consistency is the 10-second rule: draw a line through the icing with a toothpick; if it fills back in within 10 seconds, the icing is ready. Thinner than this and it will run over the outline; thicker and it will not level out before setting. The outline icing (stiff royal icing) must be fully dry before flooding, which takes 15 to 30 minutes, or the flood will break through.

content.text: The icing process is the main skill. The outline icing must dry hard before the

goes inside it; do not rush this stage. Work in batches: pipe all the outlines, let them dry for 20 minutes, then flood. Leave the flooded biscuits completely undisturbed for at least 1 hour before adding any second-layer decoration.
Beat the butter and sugar until pale and creamy. Beat in the egg and vanilla. Stir in the flour and salt until a smooth dough forms. Divide into two discs, wrap, and refrigerate for at least 1 hour.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Roll the chilled dough to 4 mm thick and cut into shapes. Place on lined trays with space between each. Bake for 10 to 14 minutes until barely golden at the edges. Cool completely on a rack before icing.

Beat the egg whites with the lemon juice and cream of tartar until frothy. Add the sifted icing sugar and beat for 3 to 5 minutes until thick, smooth, and holds stiff peaks. This is the outline icing. Transfer about two-thirds to a piping bag. Thin the remainder with water, a teaspoon at a time, until it passes the 10-second flood test. Pipe outlines around the biscuits, let dry for 20 minutes, then flood inside the outlines. Leave flat for at least 1 hour until fully set.
